yaml.title: Exploratory Sales Analysis
yaml.description: Cleaning and exploring a retail dataset to surface seasonal trends and product performance using Pandas and Plotly.

markdown.markdown:
```{=html}
Python Pandas Plotly EDA
``` Every retail business generates transaction logs — thousands of rows tracking what sold, when, and where. The problem is rarely a lack of data. It's knowing what to look at first. This project takes a synthetic retail dataset and works through a structured EDA process: cleaning the data, understanding its shape, and then asking progressively sharper questions until a few genuinely useful patterns emerge. --- ## Dataset The dataset used here is a synthetic retail sales log with the following columns: `date`, `product`, `category`, `region`, `units_sold`, and `revenue`. It covers 12 months of transactions across four product categories. --- ## Setup & Data Loading ```{python} import pandas as pd import plotly.express as px import plotly.graph_objects as go import numpy as np # Reproducible synthetic dataset np.random.seed(42) dates = pd.date_range("2024-01-01", "2024-12-31", freq="D") categories = ["Electronics", "Apparel", "Home & Garden", "Sports"] regions = ["Dublin", "Cork", "Galway", "Limerick"] rows = [] for date in dates: for _ in range(np.random.randint(3, 8)): cat = np.random.choice(categories) base = {"Electronics": 180, "Apparel": 55, "Home & Garden": 90, "Sports": 70}[cat] seasonal = 1 + 0.3 * np.sin((date.month - 1) * np.pi / 6) rows.append({ "date": date, "category": cat, "region": np.random.choice(regions), "units_sold": int(np.random.poisson(seasonal * 5)), "revenue": round(base * seasonal * np.random.uniform(0.85, 1.15) * np.random.poisson(seasonal * 5), 2) }) df = pd.DataFrame(rows) df["month"] = df["date"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str) df["month_num"] = df["date"].dt.month print(f"Rows: {len(df):,} | Columns: {df.shape[1]}") df.head() ``` --- ## Data Quality Check Before anything else, check for nulls and confirm the data types are what you'd expect. ```{python} print("Null values:\n", df.isnull().sum()) print("\nData types:\n", df.dtypes) print(f"\nDate range: {df['date'].min().date()} → {df['date'].max().date()}") ``` No nulls, correct types, full year of coverage. Clean enough to move straight into analysis. --- ## Monthly Revenue Trend The first question: is revenue growing, declining, or seasonal? ```{python} monthly = df.groupby("month")["revenue"].sum().reset_index() monthly.columns = ["month", "total_revenue"] fig = px.line( monthly, x="month", y="total_revenue", title="Monthly Revenue — Full Year", labels={"month": "", "total_revenue": "Revenue (€)"}, markers=True, color_discrete_sequence=["#7fff6e"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535", tickangle=-30), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), hoverlabel=dict(bgcolor="#1a1a26", font_color="#e2e2e2") ) fig.update_traces(line_width=2.5) fig.show() ``` There's a clear seasonal arc — revenue builds through the summer and peaks in Q4, which lines up with Christmas trading patterns. --- ## Revenue by Category Which categories are pulling the most weight? ```{python} cat_rev = df.groupby("category")["revenue"].sum().reset_index().sort_values("revenue", ascending=True) fig = px.bar( cat_rev, y="category", x="revenue", orientation="h", title="Total Revenue by Category", labels={"category": "", "revenue": "Revenue (€)"}, color="revenue", color_continuous_scale=["#252535", "#7fff6e"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)"), coloraxis_showscale=False ) fig.show() ``` Electronics dominates by revenue, which makes sense given higher average transaction values — even if it doesn't necessarily have the highest volume. --- ## Regional Breakdown Is the Dublin market as dominant as one might assume? ```{python} region_cat = df.groupby(["region", "category"])["revenue"].sum().reset_index() fig = px.bar( region_cat, x="region", y="revenue", color="category", title="Revenue by Region and Category", labels={"region": "", "revenue": "Revenue (€)", "category": "Category"}, barmode="group", color_discrete_sequence=["#7fff6e", "#68d9ff", "#a78bfa", "#ffd166"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)"), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), legend=dict(bgcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)") ) fig.show() ``` --- ## Key Takeaways :::{.callout-note} **What this analysis found:** - Revenue follows a clear seasonal curve peaking in Q4, suggesting promotional spend should be front-loaded into Q3 to capture the ramp-up. - Electronics generates disproportionate revenue relative to its transaction count — protecting margin here matters more than volume discounts. - Regional revenue is broadly distributed, so a Dublin-only focus would miss a substantial portion of the market. ::: --- ## Reflection This project reinforced something I keep running into — the most useful output of an EDA isn't the visualisations themselves, it's the shortlist of questions they raise. Here, the seasonal pattern immediately suggests a follow-up: does the peak come from higher basket sizes, or just more transactions? That's the next query to write. The other thing worth noting is that clean data still takes time. Even with a synthetic dataset designed to be tidy, there were decisions to make about aggregation level, date formatting, and how to handle multi-category breakdowns in a way that didn't obscure the signal.

← Back to Projects
